title: How exile parts carry burdens from before you had words
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Before you had words, your body was keeping score

I remember sitting in my therapist's office at 29 years old, trying to explain a feeling I'd carried my whole life. A heaviness in my chest. A dread that showed up without warning. A voice inside that whispered I was wrong, broken, too much, not enough. I had words now. I was an adult with a vocabulary and a paycheck and a carefully constructed life. But that feeling? It wasn't from now. It was from before I had words at all.

Here's the thing. Our brains develop in a specific sequence. The parts that process language, logic, and linear time come online later. The limbic system, the amygdala, the vagus nerve, these ancient structures are fully operational before we can speak. Before we can form a coherent narrative. Before we can say "this is what happened to me" or "this is how I feel about it."

So when trauma happens early, it gets stored in the body. In sensations. In images. In wordless knowing. Bessel van der Kolk documented this extensively at the Trauma Research Foundation. He showed that traumatic memories aren't stored like regular memories. They don't have a timestamp. They don't have a story attached. They just exist in the nervous system as raw, unfiltered data. And your parts, especially the exile parts, become the carriers of that data.

Think about it. An exile part is the part of you that holds the unbearable. The shame. The terror. The abandonment. The part that got locked in a closet, metaphorically or literally, and never got let out. That part didn't have language when the wound happened. It had a body. It had a nervous system. It had a survival response. And it's been running on that original programming ever since.

You might notice this in your own life. You react to something small, a tone of voice, a slammed door, a person walking away, and suddenly you're flooded. Your chest tightens. Your throat closes. You want to disappear. Your adult brain says "this is fine, this is just a minor conflict, I'm safe." But your exile part doesn't speak adult. It speaks in body sensations and emotional flashbacks. It's still back there, before words, trying to survive.

Stay with me here. This is the part that hurts and heals in equal measure. The part that was never meant to be logical.

The exile's burden is stored in the nervous system, not the narrative

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us something crucial about how early trauma lives in the body. The vagus nerve, which runs from your brainstem through your chest and abdomen, is the superhighway of your nervous system. It picks up safety cues and danger cues before your conscious mind even registers what's happening. This is the neuroception system. It's scanning constantly. It's been scanning since before you had words.

Your exile parts are carrying the data from that early scanning. They remember the feeling of a parent's body going rigid when they were angry. They remember the silence that meant something was wrong. They remember the absence of attunement, the moments when no one came, the times when the nervous system had to figure out how to survive alone.

And here's what makes this so tricky. Because these burdens were encoded before language, they can't be talked out of existence. You can't sit down with a five-year-old part and explain, rationally, that you're safe now. That part doesn't understand your words. It understands presence. It understands regulation. It understands what it feels like when someone finally, actually, sees it.

I think about this every time I see someone beating themselves up for not being able to "just think differently" about their trauma. The wellness industry loves to tell you that your thoughts create your reality. And sure, cognitive reframing has its place. But it's not going to reach the part of you that was traumatized at 18 months old. That part doesn't have a thought loop. It has a body loop. It has a survival loop. And it needs something deeper than a positive affirmation.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He gets this. He talks about emotional flashbacks as the core experience of complex trauma, and how they bypass the thinking brain entirely. That book has helped countless people understand why they feel so flooded, so suddenly, for reasons that don't seem to make sense.

Because the exile part isn't asking you to understand it. It's asking you to feel it. To be with it. To let it know that someone is finally paying attention.

The paradox of protecting the protector from the exile

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, describes a system where protectors keep exiles locked away. The protectors are the parts that manage your life. They're the ones that say "don't get too close" or "be perfect" or "never show weakness." They're not bad parts. They're doing a job. They're keeping the exile hidden because the exile's pain feels like it could destroy you.

And here's the painful irony. The protectors are often using the same strategies that were developed before words. They learned to manage the world without language. A toddler who gets ignored learns to be quiet. A baby who doesn't get held learns not to cry. A child who gets punished for having needs learns to have no needs. These are protector strategies. They're brilliant. They kept you alive. And they're still running, decades later, on that same pre-verbal operating system.

I notice this in my own protectors all the time. There's a part of me that keeps things surface-level. That doesn't let people in too far. That changes the subject when conversations get heavy. That part learned its job when I was very small, before I could articulate what I needed. It learned that vulnerability was dangerous. It learned that showing my real feelings would lead to rejection or punishment. And it's been doing that job faithfully ever since, even though the original threat is long gone.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson. It names the dynamic that so many of us lived but couldn't describe. The feeling of being seen but not truly known. The experience of having a parent who was physically present but emotionally unavailable. That book gives language to the wordless burden that exile parts have been carrying.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty by Jonice Webb. She describes what happens when children grow up in homes where their emotional needs aren't met, not through abuse necessarily, but through absence. The exile part that carries that neglect doesn't know it was neglected. It just knows something is missing. Something feels wrong. Something is empty inside.

Wild, right? That a whole human being can walk around for decades not knowing why they feel hollow, disconnected, or different from everyone else. And all because a part of them has been carrying a burden from before they had the words to name it.

How to approach the exile without re-traumatizing

So what do you do with a part that doesn't speak your language? How do you heal something that was encoded before you could form a coherent thought?

The answer, from people like Pat Ogden and Janina Fisher who work in sensorimotor psychotherapy and trauma-informed parts work, is that you approach the body first. You don't start with the story. You start with the sensation. You notice where the exile lives in your body. Maybe it's a knot in your stomach. Maybe it's a pressure in your chest. Maybe it's a numbness in your arms. You don't need to know what happened. You just need to be present with what is.

This is hard. I won't pretend it's easy. When you turn toward the exile, the protector parts will scream. They'll tell you this is dangerous. They'll flood you with distraction, shame, or self-criticism. They'll do anything to keep you away from that raw, wordless pain. And that's okay. That's their job. You don't need to fight them. You just need to acknowledge them, thank them for their work, and ask them to step back a little so you can be with the part that needs you most.

I do this in small doses. Five minutes at a time. I sit with my hand on my chest and I say, "I'm here. I see you. I know you've been waiting a long time." I don't need to know the details. I don't need to excavate the memory. I just need to offer my presence, my adult, resourced, regulated presence, to a part that has never had anyone truly show up for it.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. It walks through the IFS model in an accessible way, and it normalizes the experience of having parts that feel overwhelming or scary. It helps you understand that no part is the enemy. Not even the exile that feels like it's drowning you. That part is just a child, frozen in time, carrying something that was never supposed to be carried alone.

The exile never needed you to fix it. It needed you to witness it.

I'm going to be honest with you. This work doesn't have a finish line. You don't heal the exile and then it's done. You build a relationship with it. You learn to recognize its signals. You show up for it again and again, even when it's inconvenient, even when you'd rather numb out or distract yourself or pretend everything is fine.

Because the exile part is not your enemy. It's not broken. It's not a problem to be solved. It's a part of you that got hurt before it could understand what was happening. And it's been waiting, sometimes for decades, for someone to finally say "I see you. I'm sorry that happened. You didn't deserve it. And you're not alone anymore."

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Maybe something shifted. Maybe your throat tightened. Maybe your eyes got wet. That's the exile. It heard you. It knows you're paying attention.

You don't have to have all the answers. You don't have to remember the exact moment the burden was placed. You just have to be willing to turn toward the part of you that has been carrying it, silently, faithfully, for all these years.

That's enough. That's more than enough. That's the beginning of real healing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker (paid link)
  • Running on Empty by Jonice Webb (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz (paid link)
